topic: Areas of Inquiry: Music Technology in the Electronic and Digital Age
subject: music

items.q: What does the time signature 6/8 indicate?
items.correct: Two beats per bar, each beat divided into three (compound duple).
items.misconception: Six beats per bar, all equal in stress.
items.why: Students count 6/8 as six equal beats. In practice it's felt as TWO dotted-crotchet beats subdivided into three quavers each - compound duple, not simple sextuple.

items.q: Which chords form a plagal cadence?
items.why: Students confuse plagal (IV-I) with perfect (V-I) because both end on I. The 'amen' at the end of hymns is the classic plagal cadence - subdominant to tonic, with a softer ecclesiastical feel.

items.q: How does a harmonic minor scale differ from a natural minor?
items.correct: The seventh degree is raised by a semitone (creating a leading note).
items.misconception: All notes are raised by a semitone.
items.why: Students think the whole scale changes. Only the 7TH is altered to create a leading note, which strengthens cadences in minor keys - producing the characteristic augmented second between 6th and 7th.
It's Leon Theremin. “Robert Moog” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Whose 1956 Gesang der Junglinge mixed boy's voice with electronics?
items.why: Correct answer: Stockhausen. “Berio” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Ableton Live first released?
items.why: Exact answer: 2001 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
